anchor.snippet: an improper integral is an extension of the notion of a definite integral
The first three forms are improper because the integrals are taken over an unbounded interval
the improper integral must be rewritten using one or more limits
Here both limits must converge to a finite value for the improper integral to be said to converge

annotations.label: Improper integral with discontinuity at endpoint

anchor.snippet: with a discontinuity of any kind
the Riemann integral can often be extended by continuity
The problem here is that the integrand is unbounded in the domain of integration
Thus this is a doubly improper integral
a limit might fail to exist, or might be infinite
an integrand is unbounded near an interior point, in which case the integral must be split at that point
An improper integral converges if the limit defining it exists
It is also possible for an improper integral to diverge to infinity
This is called divergence by oscillation

annotations.label: Improper integral with two infinite endpoints

anchor.snippet: can be defined by taking two separate limits
An example of an improper integral where both endpoints are infinite is the Gaussian integral
one can however define an improper integral in the sense of Cauchy principal value
often an integral which only exists as an improper Riemann integral will exist as a (proper) Lebesgue integral
For the Henstock–Kurzweil integral , improper integration is not necessary

An integral is (C, 0) summable precisely when it exists as an improper integral
This is an integral version of Grandi's series
